groups.name: Parks and Recreation Universe
groups.explanation: Pawnee, Indiana (location), Leslie Knope (character), Amy Poehler (actor), and 'Treat yo self' (quote/catchphrase) are all from Parks and Recreation.

groups.name: TRAP — TV Antiheroes Who Are Secretly Addicts or Criminals (But Only One Is a Woman)
groups.explanation: Nurse Jackie (Jackie Peyton — drug addict nurse), Don Draper (Mad Men — identity fraud, alcoholic), Vic Mackey (The Shield — corrupt cop), and Nancy Botwin (Weeds — drug dealer) all seem to be 'morally compromised TV protagonists' but the trap is that only Nancy Botwin and Nurse Jackie are women — players may try to group them as female antiheroes, missing that all four belong together as cable-era antiheroes whose defining flaw is a secret criminal or addictive life hidden in plain sight.

groups.name: Critically Beloved Shows Cancelled After One or Two Seasons on Basic Cable or IFC/Audience
groups.explanation: Rutherford Falls (Peacock, cancelled after season 2), Brockmire (IFC, ended after 4 seasons but cult-cancelled in spirit — actually ran 4), Patriot (Amazon, cancelled after 2 seasons with fervent cult following), and Terriers (FX, cancelled after 1 season despite critical acclaim) are all shows with passionate critical followings that were cut short and mourned by TV critics as unjustly overlooked gems.

groups.name: Shows Where the Protagonist Has a Job Involving Dead People — But Is NOT a Detective
groups.explanation: Pushing Daisies (Ned brings the dead back to life, works as a pie maker), Wonderfalls (Jaye receives messages from inanimate objects tied to fate/death), Dead Like Me (Georgia becomes a grim reaper collecting souls), and Tru Calling (Tru works in a morgue and relives days to prevent deaths) — all four protagonists have an unusual supernatural relationship with death and deceased people, but none of them is a detective, cop, or forensic investigator in the traditional sense.
